title: Ventriglia and Anor v Finance Bank Zambia Limited (SCZ/7/2/2025) [2026] ZMSC 4 (10 March 2026)

paragraphs.text:
Elder Moonga & Others -v- National Institute for Scientific and
Industrial Research: CAZ/08/018/2016

Standard Chartered Bank PLC -v- Chanda and Another: SCZ
Appeal No. 92 of 2009

Bidvest Food Zambia Limited and Others-vs- CAA Import and
Export Limited: SCZ No 5 6 of 2017

Drill Africa Company Limited -v- Daniel Mpundu Konkola:
SCZ/8/246/2016.
~
Hakainde Hichilema -v- The A-G: SCZ Appeal No. 04 of 2019

Laston Phiri -v- Tropical Research Centre: SCZ Appeal No. 5 of
2014
(
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF ZAMBIA SCZ/7/2/2025
HOLDEN AT LUSAKA
(Civil Jurisdiction)
BETWEEN
DANIELE VENTRIGLIA 1ST APPLICANT
VALERO VENTRIGLIA 2ND APPLICANT
AND
FINANCE BANK ZAMBIA LIMITED RESPONDENT
Coram Musonda, DCJ, Kaoma and Kabuka JJs
on 6th May 2025 and 10th March, 2026
For the Appellant Mr. C. Sianondo with Mr. G. Mileji both of
Malambo & Co.
For the Respondent Mr. J.P. Sangwa SC, Simeza Sangwa &
Associates
RULING ON MOTION FOR LEAVE TO APPEAL TO THE SUPREME COURT
PURSUANT TO SECTION 24 (B) OF THE SUPREME COURT ACT AS READ
WITH RULE 48(3) OF SUPREME COURT·RULES, CAP. 25.
MUSONDA DCJ delivered the Ruling of the Court
When we heard this motion, the Hon. Lq.dy Justice Kabuka was a Panel
member. The Hon. Lady Justice Kabuka having since retired, this Ruling is by
the majority of the Panel.
Cases Referred to:

Elder Moonga & Others -v- Nt;1tional Institute for Scientific and
Industrial Research: CAZ/08/018/2016

Standard Chartered Bank PLC -v- Chanda and Another: SCZ
Appeal No. 92 of 2009

Bidvest Food Zambia Limited and Others-vs- CAA Import and
Export Limited: SCZ No 56 of 2017

Drill Africa Company Limited -v- Daniel Mpundu Konkola:
SCZ/8/246/2016.
Hakainde Hichilema -v- The A-G: SCZ Appeal No. 04 of 2019

Laston Phiri -v- Tropical Research Centre: SCZ Appeal No. 5 of
2014
v
R2

Mandona Freeboy -v- Joshua Nkandu: SCZ Appeal No. 6 of 2017:
[2019) ZMSC 353
Seaford Court Estates Limited v Asher: [1949) 2KB 481
Barclays Bank -v- Quince care (1988) (reported) [1992) 4 All.E.R

10. Greer -v- Kettle: [1938) AC 156
Legislation Referred to:
The Constitution of Zambia
Court of Appeal Act No. 7 of 2016

Supreme Court Act, CAP. 25.
Other works Referred to
Chitty on Contracts, Volume 1 (Sweet and Maxwell: London)

Penn, G,A, Shea, A,M & Arora, The Law Relating to Domestic
Banking, Vol. 1 (Sweet & Maxwell: London)
1.0. INTRODUCTION

1. This motion is arising from the decision of one of our
number (Kaoma, JS) to adjourn or defer the hearing of
J
an application by the Applicants for permission to
appeal to this court fallowing the Court of Appeal's
refusal to grant the Applicants the aforementioned
procedural relief.
2.0. HISTORY AND BACKGROUND
2.1. The history and background facts to the Applicants'
continuing desire to secure permission to approach this
apex court can briefly be recounted.
2.2. A private limited company which rejoiced and still
rejoices under the name of Ital Terrazzo Limited ("ITL")
R3
had been a customer of the Respondent.
2.3. Over the course of the banker and customer
relationship between the Respondent and ITL the
former availed credit or banking facilities and other
accommodation to the latter.
2.4. For the purpose of securing the said facilities and
accommodation, the Applicants executed personal
guarantees in favour of the Respondent. In terms of
those guarantees, the Applicants guaranteed the
payment, to the Respondent, of all such sums of money
as were going to be due or owing on any account
whatsoever which was held or maintained with the
Respondent by ITL together with interest.

5. According to what we have been able to glean from the
Motion Record, the credit facilities which ITL secured
from the Respondent were subsequently assigned to a
private limited company known as Zambezi Portland
Limited.
2.6. Sometime in 2009, ITL instituted an action in the High
Court of Zambia under cause 2009/HP/0381 against
the Respondent. In terms of that action, ITL sought to
be refunded moneys which the Respondent had
R4
fraudulently recovered from it on account of the credit
facilities earlier mentioned. For completeness, the said
moneys were said to have been in excess of what was
legitimately owed by ITL to the Respondent. For the
avoidance of doubt, Messrs Zambezi Portland Cement
Limited was subsequently joined to cause
2009 /HP /0381.

7. By a letter of demand dated 13th Octa ber, 2011, the
Respondent sought to recover the following moneys
from the Applicants on account of the banking facilities
and the guarantees mentioned at 2.4 above:

USD7,274,076.06 in respect of account No.
0010301880014;

ZMK 92,225,046.87 1n respect of account No.
0010301880006;and

ZMK3,842,238,626.31 in respect of account No.
0024437900005
2.8. According to the motion record, the amounts specified
in 2.7 above were said to have been due and owing as
at 30th September, 2011.
RESPONDENT INSTITUTES LEGAL PROCEEDINGS

9. Sometime after the delivery of the demand letter which
we momentarily referred to above, the Respondent
RS
instituted cause 2011/HPC/0671 in the High Court of
Zambia against the Applicants seeking the recovery of
the moneys which we specified in 2. 7 above.

10. It is not clear from the Motion Record why Cause
2011/HPC/0671 remained in cold storage until 22nd
July, 2021 when the Applicants took out an
application, before Musona, J seeking to have this
cause set aside on account of the same having been
instituted in abuse of court process. In the alternative,
the Applicants sought to have the Cause
(201 l/HPC/0671) consolidated with Cause
2009 /HP/ 0381. As noted at 2. 6 above, cause
2009/HP/0381 was instituted by ITL against the
Respondent in 2009.
2.11. In his Ruling dated 30th November, 2021 Musona, J
observed that from what he had ascertained, the facts
and transaction in the cause which was before him,
namely, Cause 201 l/HPC/0671, were the same as
those relating to cause 2009 /HP/ 0381.
2.12. His lordship also noted that the issues which
characterised both causes could be determined in
either one of them adding that it was
R6
".. treacherous for the [Respondent] to have
commenced an action in the commercial
Division in 2011. .. ~ith full knowledge of the
matter which [had] been commenced by ITL in
2009 based on the same facts ... "
2.13. The learned Judge accordingly proceeded to dismiss
cause 2011/HPC/0671 on the basis of the same having
been instituted in abuse of court process.
2.14. Following his Order in 2.13 above, Musona, J went on
to observe that the Applicants' application to
consolidate as adverted to in 2. 10 had been rendered
redundant as a result of the dismissal of Cause
201 l/HPC/0671.
2.15. The Ruling by Musona, J was not well received by the
Respondent. Accordingly, the Respondent decided to
mount an appeal to the Court of Appeal (Makungu,
Chishimba and Muzenga, JJA) against the same on one
ground the gist of which entailed faulting the judge
(M usona, J) for having determined that cause
2011/HPC/0671 had been instituted in abuse of Court
process.
2.16. Following the filing of the Notice of Appeal in the Court
of Appeal, the Applicants filed a Notice to Raise
R7
Preliminary Issues on 7th November, 2023 the gist of
which was that the Court of Appeal had no jurisdiction
to hear the appeal because no Memorandum of Appeal
was filed in the High Court as dictated by the applicable
Court Rules thereby rendering the appeal fatally
defective and depriving the Court of Appeal of the
necessary jurisdiction to entertain the same.
2.17. When the appeal came up for hearing on 17th
November, 2023, the court heard the parties' respective
arguments in relation to the Preliminary Objection
founded on the issues adverted to in 2.16 above.
2.18. In dismissing the Preliminary Objection, the Court of
Appeal observed that both the Notice of Appeal and the
Memorandum of Appeal were filed on the same day
save that only the Notice was signed by the Registrar.
According to the Court of Appeal, the fact that the
Notice of Appeal was signed and bore the same date
which appeared on the Memorandum of Appeal should
incline everyone towards the irresistible conclusion
that the fact of the memorandum having been
unsigned was a mere omission on the part of the
Registrar.
R8
2.19. According to the Court of Appeal, the situation would
have been different had both the Notice of Appeal and
the Memorandum of Appeal not been signed by the
Registrar or had the two been filed and stamped on
different dates.
2.20. The Court further observed that the Notice was filed in
the High Court on 30th December, 2021 and that on the
same date, the memorandum was filed in the Court of
Appeal. Accordingly, the court dismissed the
Preliminary Issues.
2.21. After resolving the Preliminary Issues, the Court of
Appeal proceeded to hear the parties' respective
arguments for and against the appeal which the court
considered before rendering its Ruling the gist of which
was that the conclusion by Musona, J that the two
causes which he (M usona, J) had pronounced himself
upon were the same and were based on the same facts
or issues was erroneous. According to the Court of
Appeal, cause 2009 /HP/ 0381 and cause
2011/HPC/0671 were materially and substantively
different. The court went on to observe that no issue of
R9
abuse of court process had arisen at all as Musona, J
had determined.
2.22. In the course of delivering its Ruling, the Court of
Appeal observed that, according to the statement of
claim in cause 201 l/HPC/0671, the guarantee which
the Applicants had issued in favour of the Respondent
was an unconditional one and that the implication of
that characterisation was that the guarantors were
liable on demand and that such liability was not
dependent on default on the part of the principal
borrower (ITL).
2.23. According to the Court of Appeal, the two causes in
issue were different particularly because, by its cause,
the Respondent was seeking to pursue the Applicants
on the basis of their unconditional guarantee. The
Court accordingly set aside Musona, J's Ruling and
ordered that cause 2011/HPC/0671 be sent back to
the High Court so that it could take its normal course.
2.24. The meaning and effect of the Court of Appeal's Ruling
in 2.23 was that cause 2009/HP/0381, which was
instituted by ITL against the Respondent, was going to
be proceeded with as was cause 2011/HPC/0671
I
RlO
which had been launched by the Respondent against
the Applicants.
3.0. APPLICANTS LAUNCH APPLICATION FOR PERMISSION
TO APPEAL BEFORE COURT OF APPEAL
3.1. Following the decision of the Court of Appeal as
captured 1n para 2.23 above, the Applicants
approached the Court of Appeal seeking permission to
appeal to this court against that court's decision at para
2.23 (dismissing Musona, J's Ruling).
3.2. The Applicant's search for permission to appeal to this
court was founded on three statutory premises which
are projected in section 13(3) of the Court of Appeal Act
No. 7 of 2016 in the following terms:
i. that there are compelling reasons for the appeal to be heard
by this court;
ii. that the appeal would have reasonable prospects of success;
and
iii. that the appeal raises a point of law of public impor

7. For the avoidance of doubt, the Court of Appeal did not
consider that the anomaly around the Memorandum of
Appeal was of such significance as to impact the Court
of Appeal Act's leave threshold criteria contemplated
under section 13(3) of that statute.
3.8. Quite apart from the court of Appeal having taken the
position that none of the Applicants' proposed grounds
of appeal met any of the criterion prescribed in section
13(3) of the Court of Appeal Act for the purpose of
satisfying this court's case admission leave threshold,
that court was resoundingly unequivocal that the
appeal which the Applicants wished to launch did not
raise any issues of public importance, nor did it have
any reasonable prospects of success. To crown it all,
the court did not consider that any compelling grounds
Rl3
had been raised by the Applicants to warrant granting
them leave.
4.0. APPLICANTS RENEW APPLICATION FOR PERMISSION IN
THIS COURT
4.1. Following the Applicants' failure to secure permission to
appeal before the Court of Appeal, the duo decided to
approach this court where they premised their
continued search for that permission on the statutory
prescribed threshold which they had invoked before the
Court of Appeal.
4.2. In addition to the statutory leave threshold, the
Applicants have'fronted four (04) grounds on which they
have proposed to anchor their appeal. The four grounds
are:

The Court of Appeal erred both in law and in fact in holding
that it had jurisdiction to hear the matter in the absence of the
Memorandum of Appeal being filed into the High Court.

The Court ofA ppeal erred both in law and in fact in holding that
the commencement of the matter against the guarantors was
not an abuse of court process when there was another action
by the principal.

The Court of Appeal erred both in law and infa ct in upholding
issues which were not raised before the High Court and were
not subject of determination in the court below which include
the pre requisite to file a counter claim and that the guarantee
was unconditional.
Rl4

The Court ofA ppeal erred both in law and in fact in holding that
the guarantee was unconditional and liable on demand when
infa ct there was no guarantee on record for the court to anchor
the said finding.
4.3. The Applicants' search for permission to appeal to this
court is also supported by an Affidavit which was sworn
by learned Counsel Clavel Mbone Sianondo and filed in
this court on 10th January, 2025.
4.4. Aside from the said Affidavit, the Applicants seek to rely
on the Arguments which were also filed in court on 10th
January, 2025 to buttress the application in question.
4.5. In his supporting Affidavit, the deponent, Counsel
Clavel Mbone Sianondo, deposes, by reference to
relevant exhibits, that following the High Court
(Musona. J)'s entry, on 30th November, 2021, of a Ruling
which was favourable to the Applicants, the Respondent
set about to appeal against the same.
4.6. In pursuance of its said desire to appeal, the
Respondent's Counsel proceeded to file the relevant
Notice of Appeal on 30th December, 2021 but that the
same, that is, the Notice of Appeal, was not
accompanied with the requisite Memorandum of Appeal
as required by the Rules of Court .
•
R15

7. As the hearing of the appeal adverted to in the preceding
paragraph was approaching, Counsel for the Applicants
filed a Notice of intention to Raise preliminary issues
relative to the Respondent's appeal. In particular,
learned Counsel for the Applicants indicated in the
Arguments which were filed in support of the
aforementioned Notice that the Respondent's failure to
file the Memorandum of Appeal simultaneously with the
Notice of Appeal when the said Notice was filed rendered
the Respondent's appeal fatally defective.
4.8. For the purpose of reinforcing Counsel Sianondo's
Affidavit deposition that the non-filing of the
Memorandum of Appeal rendered the appeal fatally
defective, the Applicants made reference to the Court of
Appeal decision in Elder Moonga & Others -v- National
Institute for Scientific and Industrial Research 1 in
which that court decided that failure to file the
Memorandum of Appeal was fatal to the appeal.

9. Counsel Sianondo further deposed in his Affidavit that,
in its Ruling dismissing the preliminary issue alluded
to above, the Court of Appeal observed that the
Memorandum of Appeal in question was actually filed
R16
but that the same was not just signed by the Registrar
in the designated space and that, in the Court of
Appeal's view, the non-signing of the Memorandum
was not fatal as it was a mere omission.
4.10. Counsel Sianondo also complained, in effect, that the
Court of Appeal should have provided proper guidance
on the issue of the unsigned Memorandum of Appeal
as, in learned Counsel's view, the same should have
been the subject of a formal court application to correct
or rectify the anomaly.

11. In Counsel Sianondo 's view, the approach of the Court
of Appeal in this matter, as pitted against that Court's
approach in Elder Moonga 1 yielded incompatible and
,
irreconcilable or conflicting outcomes upon which this
court of last resort must have the last say.
4.12. Counsel Sianondo concluded his Affidavit by deposing
that the matters and issues which he had deposed to
in his Affidavit transcend this particular matter or
dispute and have the potential to generate a
snowballing effect on the general administration of
justice.
R17

13. Leaving aside the matters which were deposed to in the
affidavit of Counsel Sianondo, the Applicants' Motion
for permission to appeal is supported by Legal
Arguments which were filed into Court on 10th
January, 2025.
4.14. One argument which has been projected in the
Applicants' Arguments revolves around the defective
Memorandum of Appeal which was highlighted in
Counsel Sianondo's Affidavit.
4.15. A point which has been adverted to in the legal
Arguments around the Memorandum of Appeal in
question is that the issues which the Court of Appeal
brought to the fore and that court's reaction thereto
need to be properly and authoritatively reconciled,
given that "the Notice and Memorandum of Appeal are
the foundation of ... all appeals".
4.16. According to the Applicants, 'conflicting' and/ or
'irreconcilable' issues around the Notice and
Memorandum of Appeal, as the very 'fountain head'
and ''foundation" of appeals raise issues of law of public
importance which transcend whosoever may be parties
to an action at any point in time.
Rl8
4.17. Another point of note which the Applicants raised in
relation to the Memorandum of Appeal in issue was
that nothing was pronounced by the Court of Appeal in
the way of guidance or corrective measures for the
purpose of regularising the anomaly. Instead, the
appeal in question proceeded right up to hearing
without anything being done about it. In this regard,
Counsel for the Applicants cited the case of Standard
Chartered Bank PLC -v- Chanda and Another2 in
which this court observed that:
"[A] reason, no matter how well
articulated, cannot, of its own; cure a
defect. The party concerned must [take
appropriate measures] to cure a defect ...
The court has no mandate to choose to
ignore the defect [let alone] to proceed as
if the defect never existed".
4.18. Another issue around which the Applicants' Counsel
loudly complained revolved around the Court of
Appeal's decision to:
"[Go] ahead to determine the matter
based on the pleadings and without trial
.. . on the aspect that the guarantees
were unconditional, an issue which was
not the subject of determination before
the High Court ... "
R19
4.19. Having regard to what has been highlighted in 4.18, the
Applicants' Counsel remonstrated over the lower
court's postulation around "a conditional and an
unconditional ... guarantee" and insisted that this is
an issue of great public importance with serious
implications for commerce in our jurisdiction and,
therefore, one that the Supreme Court should
interrogate and pronounce itself on.
4.20. By way of summing up, the Applicants' counsel has
contended that the issues which the Applicants have
highlighted in this motion transcend this particular
matter as well as the parties involved and are deserving
of this final court's attention and interrogation so that
the litigating public can be appropriately guided.
4.21. Turning to the Respondent, the Motion Record before
us does not appear to contain any opposing Affidavit to
the Motion by or behalf of the Respondent. However,
very detailed 'Skeleton Arguments' were filed on behalf
of the Respondent in the way of opposing the Motion.
4.22. Foremost, the Respondent has contended in its
Arguments that the Applicants' motion is devoid of
merit and ought to be dismissed.
R20
4.23. The Respondent has projected, by way of an
overarching contention, that applications for
permission to appeal to this court from a decision of
the Court of Appeal are governed or regulated by
section 24(b) of the Supreme Court Act and not section
13(3) of the Court of Appeal Act and this court's
decision in Bidvest Food Zambia Limited and
Others-vs- CAA Import and Export Limited3
•
4.24. While deferring to its overarching contention in 4.23,
the Respondent contends that in the event of this court
disagreeing with its postulation in 4.23, it remains the
position of the Respondent that the Applicants' search
for permission to come to this court should fail on the
footing that the Applicants' proposed grounds of appeal
offend Order 10, r 9(2) of the Court of Appeal Rules.
4.25. Having projected the Respondent's overarching
contentions in 4.23 and 4.24 above, learned counsel
for the Respondent has gone further to avail some flesh
to his overarching contentions by positing that, in spite
of moving this court pursuant to section 24(b) of the
Supreme Court Act as read with Rule 48(3) of the Rules
of the Supreme Court, CAP. 25, the Applicants have
R21
not advanced any arguments which address the
provisions we momentarily alluded to.
4.26. According to the Respondent, what the Applicants have
only done, in aid of their application, is to demonstrate
that they have satisfied the requirements of section
13(3) of the Court of Appeal Act, to the extent that this
provision was unpacked and given life in our seminal
decision in BIDVEST3
•
4.27. The Respondent has gone further to contend that, so
far as the issue of seeking permission to appeal is
concerned, the Court of Appeal is governed by its own
legal provision, namely, section 13(3) of the Court of
Appeal Act, while this court is separately regulated by
section 24(b) of the Supreme Court Act.
4.28. The Respondent has also argued that, so far as this
court is concerned, the exercise of its discretionary
power in favour of or against the granting of permission
to appeal depends on the ability of the party seeking
such permission to demonstrate that their proposed
appeal has prospects of success.
4.29. According to the Respondent, a party seeking
permission to appeal from the Court of Appeal to this
R22
court is required to fulfill or satisfy the statutory
thresholds which are embedded in section 13(3) of the
Court of Appeal Act.
4.30. The Respondent has further contended that the
application for permission to appeal to this court which
the Applicants mounted, to the extent that the same
was founded on section 13(3) of the Court of Appeal
Act, is ultra-vires section 24(b) of the Supreme Court
Act.
4.31. The Re

7. For the avoidance of doubt, the Court of Appeal said:
"We note that the Memorandum of Appeal
was indeed not signed or endorsed by the
Registrar. We however note that the Notice
of Appeal was filed on the 30th December,
2016 in the High Court, signed by the
Registrar and filed in the High Court on the
R29
same date. The Memorandum of Appeal was
equally filed in this court on the same day.
We also note that a Notice of Appeal and
Memorandum of Appeal were filed at the same
time. The fact that the Notice of Appeal was
signed and the date on it is the same as the
one on the Memorandum of Appeal leads to
an irresistible conclusion that the failure to
sign the Memorandum of Appeal was an
omission on the part of the Registrar".
5.8. In their Arguments, the Applicants complained that
what the Court of Appeal said, as captured in 5.7 above,
was neither enough nor helpful to the litigating public,
be it now or in the future. The Applicants opined that
the Court of Appeal should have done better, as for
instance, by facilitating the rectification of the anomaly
which had characterised the memorandum. To reinforce
this point, the Applicants referred us to the case of
Standard Chartered Bank Zambia PLC -v- Chanda
and Another2 where this Court said:
"We have stated in a plethora of cases that [a]
reason, no matter how well articulated,
cannot of its own, cure a defect. The party
concerned must [take appropriate measures]
to cure a defect. The court has no mandate
to choose to ignore the defect [let alone] to
proceed as if the defect never existed".
R30
5.9. We must immediately confirm that we do associate
ourselves with the Applicants' protestations. Clearly,
the lackadaisical or even casual manner in which the
Court of Appeal approached the Registrar's failure to
sign or endorse the Memorandum of Appeal left much
to be desired particularly in the light of that senior
Court's acknowledgement that there had been an
'omission' on the part of the Registrar.
We pause here to remind ourselves of a variety of
troubling issues and questions which freely flow from
the Court of Appeal's apparent disposition: Was the
appeal whose Record of Appeal contained the defective
or irregular Memorandum of Appeal to be treated as
·competent for the purpose of its prosecution? Was the
'Certificate of Record' which the Registrar prepared and
is part of the Record and which announces that the
Record of Appeal had been prepared in accordance with
the Rules of Court to be relied upon? ·
5.10. As the Applicants correctly argued, a valid
Memorandum of Appeal is the foundation stone on
which an appeal to this Court rests. It is, without
R31
doubt, not a document that can be trivialized in the
manner the subject Memorandum of Appeal was
apparently treated by the Court of Appeal which,
following its creation by the Republican Constitution,
as amended in 2016, now bears the primary
responsibility for setting normative standards for the
conduct of civil litigation.
5.11. The view which this Court takes is that Court of Appeal
should have reflected more patiently and more
profoundly on the necessity of having the situation
which had arisen pronounced upon by this court not
only on account of its gravity but also on account of the
fact that it transcended the interests of the particular
parties who were appearing before the court at the
time. Needless to say, the Court below should have
also recognised that the issue of having an appeal or a
purported appeal in this court of last resort that would
be riding on a defective or invalid Memorandum of
Appeal is so grave that it goes to the competence of the
appeal itself, let alone, the Court of Appeal's own
jurisdiction to entertain it! In truth, the manner in
which the Court of Appeal dealt with the Memorandum
R32
in issue went against both the letter and spirit of the
guidance we proffered in Standard Chartered Bank
Zambia PLC2 For the removal of any doubt, the Court
•
chose to ignore the defect, to treat the defect with the
memorandum as if it never existed, even in the face of
our clear guidance that a court does not have such
liberty or 'mandate'.
5.12. All said, what the Applicants fashioned as the first
ground of Appeal is deserving of this court's attention
and interrogation, because, in truth, the issue it raises
is one of public importance.
5.13. In terms of the Applicants' intended appeal's prospects
of success, we are of the strong view that the proposed
ground, which is numbered 4 in the summons/motion
for leave, is poignantly instructive.
5.14. In its judgment dated 24th November 2023, the Court
below (Makungu, Chishimba and Muzenga JJA) made
the following observations, at page J13:
"We ... agree that according to the Statement
of Claim the guarantee undertaken (sic.) by
the Appellants was an unconditional one,
where the guarantor is liable on demand. It
is not therefore dependent on default by the
principal borrower.
R33
Whether or not' the appellant was rightly
entitled to assert the claim on demand from
guarantors is an issue for determination in
the cause
We therefore find that the causes of action
are different, because in casu, the appellant
is pursuing the guarantors on the basis of the
unconditional guarantee".
5.15. To put the above quotation into context, we return to
the 'History and Background' issues which we
highlighted under para. 2 above.
It is to be noted from the background issues that the
Applicants had guaranteed some borrowings and other
accommodation by a company called Ital Terrazzo
Limited from the Respondent.
5.16. It should further be noted that the portion or passage
which has been drawn from the judgment of the Court
of Appeal which the Applicants propose to appeal
against is completely misleading and does not reflect
the correct position of the law.
5.17. It is also quite clear and self-evident that the passage
from the Court of Appeal's judgment which has been
reproduced at para 5.13 appears to have been informed
R34
by what is contained at p
